author: @Palmit_
text: i think my ex-girlfriend had one of those.  or something very similar. hers ran on diesel from what i could hear .. 🤔

replies.author: @jaxjax2011
replies.text: Stirling engines run on heat differentials.

replies.author: @Palmit_
replies.text: @jaxjax2011 so you're saying it was  probably peltier device?

replies.author: @jaxjax2011
replies.text: @Palmit_ No. Peltier devices convert thermal into electric energy using the piezoelectric effect. Stirling engines convert thermal into mechanical energy using resonant expansion/compression cycles. If you put the reciprocating metal thing inside a solenoid it could generate electricity.

replies.author: @Palmit_
replies.text: @jaxjax2011 hol dup. are you saying .. let me get this straight..that velocity is directly proportional to the energy applied divided by mass squared? and that the energy is actually self-reciprocating in some ways?

replies.author: @jaxjax2011
replies.text: @Palmit_ Yes? The heat being applied to the bottom of the can causes the gas inside to expand, which pushes up on the rubber, which pushed back down and re-compresses the gas, which then heats and expands again. Because there is constant heat energy being added each expansion/compression cycle is essentially converting heat energy into mechanical energy. The gas experiences resistance as it expands which cools it. I'm not sure if self-reciprocating is the right phrase though. If no heat is added, this process will die out due to friction and radiating heat. If you add extra drag by trying to extract a current then it will stop even more quickly. The reciprocation I refer to is just to say that it happens in a cycle, not that there is some regenerative effect.
